defs: An East Indian insectivore (Tupaia ferruginea). It is arboreal in its habits, and has a bushy tail. The fur is soft, and varies from rusty red to maroon and to brownish black.

records.ety: [Corrupt. fr. prest ready money advanced, a loan; hence, earnest money given soldiers on entering service. See Prest, n.]

defs: To force into service, particularly into naval service; to impress.
quotes: To peaceful peasant to the wars is pressed.

defs: A commission to force men into public service, particularly into the navy.
quotes: I have misused the king's press.

records.ety: [F. presser, fr. L. pressare to press, fr. premere, pressum, to press. Cf. Print, v.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Pressed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Pressing.]

defs: To urge, or act upon, with force, as weight; to act upon by pushing or thrusting, in distinction from pulling; to crowd or compel by a gradual and continued exertion; to bear upon; to squeeze; to compress; as, we press the ground with the feet when we walk; we press the couch on which we repose; we press substances with the hands, fingers, or arms; we are pressed in a crowd.
quotes: Good measure, pressed down, and shaken together.

defs: To squeeze, in order to extract the juice or contents of; to squeeze out, or express, from something.
quotes: From sweet kernels pressed, She tempers dulcet creams.
And I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand.

defs: To squeeze in or with suitable instruments or apparatus, in order to compact, make dense, or smooth; as, to press cotton bales, paper, etc.; to smooth by ironing; as, to press clothes.
To embrace closely; to hug.
quotes: Leucothoe shook at these alarms, And pressed Palemon closer in her arms.

defs: To oppress; to bear hard upon.
quotes: Press not a falling man too far.

defs: To straiten; to distress; as, to be pressed with want or hunger.
To exercise very powerful or irresistible influence upon or over; to constrain; to force; to compel.
quotes: Paul was pressed in the spirit, and testified to the Jews that Jesus was Christ.

defs: To try to force (something upon some one); to urge or inculcate with earnestness or importunity; to enforce; as, to press divine truth on an audience.
quotes: He pressed a letter upon me within this hour.
Be sure to press upon him every motive.

defs: To drive with violence; to hurry; to urge on; to ply hard; as, to press a horse in a race.
quotes: The posts . . . went cut, being hastened and pressed on, by the king's commandment.
notes: ☞ Press differs from drive and strike in usually denoting a slow or continued application of force; whereas drive and strike denote a sudden impulse of force.

defs: To exert pressure; to bear heavily; to push, crowd, or urge with steady force.
To move on with urging and crowding; to make one's way with violence or effort; to bear onward forcibly; to crowd; to throng; to encroach.
quotes: They pressed upon him for to touch him.

defs: To urge with vehemence or importunity; to exert a strong or compelling influence; as, an argument presses upon the judgment.

An apparatus or machine by which any substance or body is pressed, squeezed, stamped, or shaped, or by which an impression of a body is taken; sometimes, the place or building containing a press or presses.
notes: ☞ Presses are differently constructed for various purposes in the arts, their specific uses being commonly designated; as, a cotton press, a wine press, a cider press, a copying press, etc. See Drill press.

defs: Specifically, a printing press.

The art or business of printing and publishing; hence, printed publications, taken collectively, more especially newspapers or the persons employed in writing for them; as, a free press is a blessing, a licentious press is a curse.
An upright case or closet for the safe keeping of articles; as, a clothes press.
The act of pressing or thronging forward.
quotes: In their throng and press to that last hold.

defs: Urgent demands of business or affairs; urgency; as, a press of engagements.
A multitude of individuals crowded together; <?/ crowd of single things; a throng.
quotes: They could not come nigh unto him for the press.
